fun_fact: PE is the most common plastic, making up over 30% of global plastic production.
PVC was synthesized accidentally in 1872 when vinyl chloride gas was left in the sun.
Styrofoam is just expanded Polystyrene, consisting of 98% air!
When spun into fibers, PET is commonly known as 'Polyester' in the clothing industry.
Teflon is so slippery that geckos cannot stick to it!
Nylon sparked the 'Nylon Riots' of 1940 when millions of women rushed to buy stockings.
PP has incredible fatigue resistance, making it perfect for 'living hinges' on Tic-Tac boxes.
PC is used to make astronaut helmet visors because of its extreme impact resistance.
Commonly known as Plexiglass, PMMA transmits more light than standard glass.
PU is the core ingredient in memory foam mattresses and bowling balls.
PDMS is the main component in Silly Putty, giving it viscoelastic properties.
PLA is derived from fermented plant starch, usually corn or sugarcane, instead of petroleum.
ABS is so tough that LEGO bricks can withstand up to 4,240 Newtons of force before breaking.
Once cured, Epoxy undergoes a chemical reaction that makes it permanently cross-linked.
Kevlar is 5 times stronger than steel on an equal weight basis.
Cellulose is the most abundant organic polymer on Earth.
Natural rubber is tapped directly from the Hevea brasiliensis tree as latex.
Neoprene was the first mass-produced synthetic rubber, invented by DuPont in 1930.
PEG is biologically inert and is used as the lipid coating in mRNA vaccines.
PVA is the polymer used in Tide Pods because it completely dissolves in water.
POM has highly crystalline structure giving it incredibly low friction.
PEEK is so biologically inert and strong that it is used to replace human bones.
PVDF is highly piezoelectric, meaning it generates electricity when squeezed!
Almost all modern Carbon Fiber is made by baking PAN at extremely high temperatures.
PBT is heavily used to make high-quality mechanical keyboard caps because it resists shine.
SBR accounts for more than 50% of all synthetic rubber produced globally.
Unlike natural rubber, NBR is highly resistant to oils, greases, and gasoline.
Invented in 1907, Bakelite was the world's very first fully synthetic plastic.
Chitosan is made by treating the discarded shells of shrimp and crabs!
If you have ever used Elmer's white glue, you were using liquid PVAc.
EVA foam is the standard material used to make cosplay armor and Crocs shoes.
PPS is highly flame retardant and resists virtually all chemicals under 200°C.
Ultem has a distinctive amber transparency and is heavily used in aerospace engineering.
Because its melting point is 60°C, you can melt PCL in hot water and mold it by hand.
PGA was the very first totally synthetic absorbable suture material.
Alginate is extracted directly from the cell walls of brown algae (seaweed).
Unlike most polymers, its backbone consists of alternating Silicon and Oxygen atoms, not Carbon.
Melamine resin is the super-hard coating used on Formica countertops.
It is widely used as a structural glue in particleboard and MDF furniture.
PBI has the highest thermal stability of any commercially available polymer.
It can bind up to 1000 times its weight in water, making it a powerful natural moisturizer.
During WWII, PVP was heavily used as a blood plasma substitute to save lives.
PANI was discovered in the mid-19th century as a dye, long before it was known to conduct electricity.
P3HT is heavily researched for flexible, transparent solar panels.
By changing the ratio of PLA to PGA, scientists can control exactly how fast this polymer dissolves in the body.
PEI acts like a Trojan Horse, helping deliver synthetic DNA directly into living cells.
Torlon is one of the strongest melt-processable plastics available on the market.
PES membranes are used to filter human blood during dialysis treatments.
This copolymer is essential for holding together the electrodes in your smartphone's battery.
Inspired by the adhesive proteins that mussels use to stick to wet rocks!
PNIPAM collapses into a tight coil when heated past 32°C, squeezing out water like a sponge.
PVB is the invisible layer inside your car's windshield that stops glass from shattering inward.
A single gram of PAA can absorb up to 300 grams of water!
SBS behaves like rubber at room temperature but can be melted and molded like plastic.
PHAs dissolve safely in ocean water, making them a huge hope for stopping marine plastic pollution.
This specific blend provides the perfect groove acoustics required for classic vinyl records.
PEEK can withstand 343°C and is replacing titanium in many modern aircraft designs.
PPO is rarely used pure; it's almost always blended with Polystyrene to make it moldable.
LCPs remain highly ordered in the melt state, behaving both like a crystal and a liquid.
PVDC was originally marketed as 'Saran Wrap' because of its extreme impermeability to oxygen.
PEN is the stronger, stiffer, and more temperature-resistant cousin to PET.
Many PTT fibers have a natural 'zig-zag' shape at the molecular level, making carpets springy.
PBAT breaks down fully in soil within weeks, making it a favorite for eco-friendly farming.
Used in advanced nano-engineering to build microscopic drug delivery capsules layer by layer.
Super Glue cures instantly when it reacts with the trace amounts of water vapor in the air!
PMP is one of the lightest known solid plastics, and it transmits UV light exceptionally well.
EPDM is incredibly resistant to ozone and UV light, surviving on roofs for over 50 years.
Hypalon was the preferred material for military Zodiac boats due to its extreme toughness.
PTMEG is the chemical foundation for making the incredibly stretchy fibers in yoga pants.
When quaternized, PVPyr can instantly kill bacteria that come into contact with its surface.
PTs can instantly change color when an electrical voltage is applied to them.
Because of its simple linear structure, HDPE packs tightly and is incredibly strong.
LDPE has long molecular branches that prevent it from packing tightly, making it soft and flexible.
UHMWPE is so tough it is used to replace human hip joints and line dump truck beds.
Unlike Nylon 6-6, Nylon 6 is synthesized by ring-opening polymerization, not condensation.
Nylon 11 is a true bioplastic, derived primarily from castor beans rather than petroleum.
Nylon 12 absorbs the least amount of moisture of all commercial polyamides.
PSU can withstand repeated steam sterilization in autoclaves without losing its strength.
PPSU is nearly unbreakable, enduring thousands of sterilization cycles without cracking.
PEKK can be 3D printed to exactly match a patient's skull shape for cranial reconstruction.
EVOH has such high barrier properties that it keeps ketchup from oxidizing and turning brown in plastic bottles.
COC looks like glass but is shatterproof, making it perfect for holding liquid medicines.
Because of its perfectly alternating molecular structure, sPS is crystalline and melts at a blazing 270°C.
Surlyn is an ionomer, meaning it contains metallic ions that give it incredible cut-resistance on golf balls.
PB-1 creeps less than PE or PP, making it ideal for continuous high-pressure plumbing.
Under intense UV radiation, PAR undergoes a rearrangement that actually makes it more weather resistant!
pHEMA was the material used to invent the world's very first soft contact lenses in Prague.
BMI composite matrices allow stealth fighters like the F-22 to withstand extreme aerodynamic heating.
Vinyl ester bridges the gap between polyester and epoxy, offering high strength at a lower cost.
UPR combined with chopped glass fiber is the global standard for building recreational boat hulls.
Novolac is a critical ingredient in the photolithography process that prints circuits onto silicon chips.
This resin acts as the glue that permanently bonds the rubber of a tire to its internal steel belts.
DAP maintains excellent electrical insulation even in 100% humidity environments.
Furan resins are derived from agricultural by-products like oat hulls and corncobs.
Viton O-rings are used in jet engines because they shrug off extreme heat and corrosive aviation fuels.
FFKM has the resilience of rubber but the nearly indestructible chemical resistance of Teflon.
Polybutadiene is the rubber that gives superballs and golf balls their incredible bounce.
Adding chlorine to PE randomly disrupts its crystals, turning a stiff plastic into a flexible rubber.
TPU is highly abrasion-resistant, which is why it is used to make durable skateboard wheels.
Pebax foam is the secret behind the explosive energy return in modern marathon 'super shoes'.
ECO has excellent resistance to modern biofuels and does not build up static electricity.
ACM can withstand hot automatic transmission fluid at 150°C without degrading.
FVMQ combines the extreme low-temperature flexibility of silicone with the fuel resistance of fluorocarbons.
Agarose forms a microscopic 3D mesh that biologists use to sort fragments of DNA by size.
Extracted from red edible seaweeds, carrageenan is heavily used to thicken almond milk and ice cream.
Pectin is the natural structural acid found in apple peels that makes fruit jams gel.
Certain lactic acid bacteria produce dextran to build the sticky plaque found on human teeth.

applications: Salad Dressing Stabilizer; Oil Drilling Fluid
fun_fact: Xanthan gum is highly shear-thinning—it gets thinner when shaken, which is why ketchup pours out of the bottle easily.
Collagen makes up 25% to 35% of the whole-body protein content in mammals.
Gelatin is simply hydrolyzed collagen, broken down so it dissolves in hot water to make Jell-O.
Spider silk fibroin is tougher than Kevlar and highly researched for advanced bulletproof armors.
Keratin is the extraordinarily tough structural protein that makes up human hair, fingernails, and rhino horns.
PGA is naturally produced by Bacillus bacteria in natto (fermented soybeans) and gives it its stringy texture.
The discovery that doped polyacetylene could conduct electricity won the 2000 Nobel Prize in Chemistry.
PPP is essentially a chain of linked benzene rings, making it exceptionally rigid and stable.
Polycarbazole derivatives are actively used in designing the hole-transport layers for modern perovskite solar cells.
PEDOT is highly transparent, making it perfect for the touch-sensitive layers on smartphone screens.

applications: Chewing Gum Base; Tire Inner Liners
fun_fact: PIB is the primary synthetic rubber used to give modern chewing gum its long-lasting elasticity.
Polyvinyl ethers are extremely tacky and remain sticky even at very low temperatures.
PHB is naturally produced by bacteria as a way to store energy!
PBS decomposes naturally into water and CO2 in soil within a few months.
PPy can absorb radar waves making it useful for stealth aircraft.
PPV was the first polymer discovered to emit light for OLEDs.
PVF is incredibly weather resistant and protects solar panels for decades.
PCTFE has the lowest water vapor transmission rate of any plastic.
PEA is the chemical secret that makes many pressure-sensitive tapes sticky.
PBA remains flexible even at extremely low temperatures.
PMA is often chewed—it is a common base for chewing gum!
This polymer hardens when exposed to UV light!
PEO is water-soluble and is used to make solid battery electrolytes.
PPOx is the main building block for memory foam mattresses.
PVFm is primarily used to coat magnet wires in electric motors.
PMAA changes its shape depending on the pH level of its environment.
PVSA conducts protons efficiently making it vital for hydrogen fuel cells.
Its backbone is made of Phosphorus and Nitrogen instead of Carbon!
PDPS has a very high refractive index making it great for optical lenses.
Polyfluorenes are the main polymers used to create blue light in OLED TVs.
PPC is brilliantly synthesized using captured greenhouse gas (Carbon Dioxide)!
PDO sutures dissolve perfectly inside the human body over 6 months.
POEs erode strictly from the surface making drug release highly predictable.
Used to deliver chemotherapy directly to tumors after surgery.
PVAm is highly reactive and is used to increase the strength of paper.
POx is a pseudopeptide being researched as an alternative to PEG in medicine.
PVCap becomes completely insoluble in water when it gets too warm!
The Beijing National Aquatics Center (Water Cube) is entirely clad in ETFE bubbles.
FEP is very similar to Teflon but is melt-processable, making it easier to mold.
PFA provides almost identical chemical resistance to PTFE but is completely clear.
Nomex cannot melt or drip; it only chars at extremely high temperatures.
Kapton tape remains stable from -269°C to +400°C, making it essential for NASA.

applications: Mars rover parachute cords; Body armor
fun_fact: Zylon is significantly stronger than Kevlar and was used in the Mars Perseverance rover.
PVK was the first organic polymer discovered to have photoconductive properties.

applications: Water softening; Hole transport in OLEDs
fun_fact: PSS is usually mixed with PEDOT to create transparent, conductive coatings for flexible screens.
Cross-linked PAM forms a gel that is standardly used for DNA and protein separation in labs.
PDMAEMA is both temperature and pH responsive, making it a highly 'intelligent' plastic.

applications: Root canal fillings; Antique golf balls
fun_fact: Unlike highly elastic natural rubber, Gutta-percha is rigid and solid at room temperature.
pDCPD is formed via Reaction Injection Molding (RIM), curing from a liquid to a solid in just seconds.
Butyl rubber is exceptionally impermeable to gases, which is why it keeps your tires inflated.
Adding chlorine to butyl rubber makes it cure faster and heavily resist ozone degradation.
PEK has an incredibly high continuous use temperature, surviving environments up to 250°C.
It protects medicine from stomach acid but dissolves perfectly once it reaches the intestines.
This polymer has natural ferroelectric properties, meaning it has a switchable electrical polarization.
A highly charged cationic polymer used to clump floating dirt particles in drinking water.
It combines the strength of PLA with the rubbery elasticity of PCL for growing new heart valves.
It acts as an excellent dielectric (insulating) layer in printed, flexible electronics.
Unlike branched PEI, linear PEI is solid at room temperature and is a gold standard for cell transfection.
PMI foam (Rohacell) is one of the lightest, stiffest structural core materials in aerospace.
Used as a low-toxicity binder in specialty printing inks.
PFPE (Krytox) is a liquid polymer that will not burn, even in 100% liquid oxygen environments!
PCHMA has almost zero water absorption, making it highly dimensionally stable for optics.
